title: Information Security Governance
description: Understand security governance structures and management responsibilities

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Security governance ensures security aligns with business objectives. Without proper governance, security becomes either an impediment to business or an afterthought that fails to protect.

sections.title: Security Governance Framework
sections.content:
**Key Governance Elements:**

**Strategic Direction**
• Security strategy aligned with business
• Risk appetite definition
• Resource allocation
• Performance measurement

**Organizational Structure**
• Clear roles and responsibilities
• Reporting relationships
• Accountability framework
• Segregation of duties

**Policy Framework**
• Security policies
• Standards and procedures
• Guidelines
• Enforcement mechanisms

**Oversight**
• Board involvement
• Management steering
• Audit and assurance
• Continuous improvement

sections.title: Security Policy Hierarchy
sections.content:
**Policy Documentation Levels:**

**Policies**
• High-level statements
• Management intent
• Mandatory
• Reviewed annually

**Standards**
• Specific requirements
• Measurable criteria
• Technology-specific
• Mandatory

**Procedures**
• Step-by-step instructions
• How to implement
• Role-specific
• Updated frequently

**Guidelines**
• Recommendations
• Best practices
• Optional/advisory
• Flexible

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Policy Hierarchy
sections.content:
**P-S-P-G:**
• **P**olicy = High-level what (mandatory)
• **S**tandards = Specific how much (mandatory)
• **P**rocedures = Detailed how-to (mandatory)
• **G**uidelines = Recommended way (optional)

*Policies are strategic; Procedures are tactical*

sections.title: Security Metrics
sections.content:
**Measuring Security Effectiveness:**

**Operational Metrics**
• Incidents detected/resolved
• Patch compliance percentage
• Vulnerability remediation time
• Access review completion

**Risk Metrics**
• Risk exposure trends
• Control effectiveness
• Audit findings
• Compliance status

**Program Metrics**
• Awareness training completion
• Policy acknowledgment
• Budget utilization
• Project delivery

**Reporting:**
• Dashboard for executives
• Detailed for operations
• Trend analysis
• Benchmarking

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Security governance aligns security with business objectives
Structure includes CISO, managers, data owners, custodians
Policy hierarchy: policies, standards, procedures, guidelines
Policies and standards are mandatory; guidelines are advisory
Metrics measure operational effectiveness and risk posture

knowledgeCheck.question: An organization's security strategy should PRIMARILY be aligned with:
options: The latest industry security trends and technologies.
Compliance requirements outlined by regulatory bodies.
The organization's overall business objectives and risk appetite.
The security team's preferred security frameworks and tools.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A security strategy that is not aligned with business objectives and risk appetite will likely be ineffective and a waste of resources. While industry trends and compliance are important, they should support the business goals, not dictate them.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the ultimate goal of information security from a business perspective.

reveal.question: What are the three levels of policy documentation, in order from most general to most specific?

rows.label: Purpose
values: Define specific requirements and measurable criteria.
Provide step-by-step instructions on how to implement a standard or policy.

rows.label: Scope
values: Often technology-specific and mandatory for compliance.
Role-specific and detail the exact steps to be taken.

rows.label: Example
values: All servers must be patched with the latest security updates within 72 hours of release.
To patch a server, follow these steps: 1. Download the patch... 2. Test the patch in a non-production environment... 3. Apply the patch to the production server...

title: Data Classification and Protection
description: Learn data classification schemes and protection requirements

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Not all data is equal. Classification enables appropriate protection - too little protection risks breaches, too much wastes resources. Classification is the foundation of data protection.

sections.title: Classification Process
sections.content:
**Classification Steps:**

**1. Inventory**
• Identify data assets
• Determine data types
• Map data locations
• Document data flows

**2. Classify**
• Apply classification criteria
• Data owner assigns level
• Consider regulatory requirements
• Document decisions

**3. Label**
• Mark documents/files
• Metadata tagging
• Visual indicators
• Automated tools

**4. Handle**
• Apply appropriate controls
• Storage requirements
• Transmission security
• Disposal procedures

sections.title: Classification Criteria
sections.content:
**How to Determine Classification:**

**Sensitivity Factors:**
• Competitive value
• Personal information
• Legal/regulatory requirements
• Contractual obligations

**Impact Assessment:**
• Financial impact if disclosed
• Reputational damage
• Regulatory penalties
• Operational disruption

**Examples:**
• **Confidential**: Trade secrets, customer PII, financial data
• **Internal**: Internal memos, project plans, general business
• **Public**: Marketing materials, press releases, public filings

sections.title: Data Protection Controls
sections.content:
**Protection by Classification:**

**Confidential Data:**
• Encryption at rest and in transit
• Strict access control
• Audit logging
• DLP monitoring
• Secure disposal

**Internal Data:**
• Access control
• Employee only access
• Standard encryption
• Controlled distribution

**Public Data:**
• Integrity controls
• Version control
• Minimal access restrictions
• Standard handling

sections.title: Data Loss Prevention (DLP)
sections.content:
**DLP Components:**

**Network DLP**
• Monitor network traffic
• Email scanning
• Web uploads
• Protocol inspection

**Endpoint DLP**
• USB controls
• Print monitoring
• Clipboard protection
• Application control

**Storage DLP**
• File share scanning
• Database discovery
• Cloud storage
• Data at rest

**Actions:**
• Alert
• Block
• Quarantine
• Encrypt

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Classification levels: public, internal, restricted, confidential
Data owner is responsible for classification decisions
Protection controls increase with classification level
Classification process: inventory, classify, label, handle
DLP monitors data at network, endpoint, and storage levels

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is the MOST critical step in ensuring the effectiveness of a data classification program?
options: A. Implementing a Data Loss Prevention (DLP) solution.
B. Establishing clear and well-defined classification criteria.
C. Providing comprehensive training to all employees on data handling procedures.
D. Conducting regular penetration testing to identify vulnerabilities.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: While all options are important, establishing clear and well-defined classification criteria (B) is the foundational step. Without it, DLP solutions lack context, training is ineffective, and penetration testing results are difficult to interpret in terms of data sensitivity.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about what must be in place *before* you can effectively implement any of the other controls.

reveal.question: List the three primary steps in the Data Classification Process in the correct order.
reveal.answer: 1. Inventory: Identify, type, locate, and map data. 2. Classify: Apply criteria, assign level, consider regulations, and document. 3. Label: Mark documents/files accordingly.

rows.label: Encryption
values: Encryption at rest and in transit

knowledgeCheck.question: An organization is implementing a data classification program. During the impact assessment phase, they identify a dataset containing customer credit card information. If this data were disclosed, it would result in significant financial penalties and reputational damage. According to the data protection principles discussed, which classification level would be MOST appropriate for this data?
options: A. Public
B. Internal
C. Confidential
D. Restricted
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Confidential (C) is the most appropriate classification. The scenario highlights significant financial and reputational impact, aligning with the characteristics of confidential data requiring the highest level of protection. While 'Restricted' might seem appropriate, 'Confidential' is the more commonly used term in data classification frameworks for data with this level of sensitivity.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the severity of the impact described in the scenario.

title: Access Control Fundamentals
description: Master the principles and models of access control

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Access control is the gatekeeper of information security. It ensures only authorized users can access resources they need. Weak access control is a leading cause of breaches.

sections.title: AAA Framework
sections.content:
**Authentication, Authorization, Accounting:**

**Authentication**
• Verifies identity
• "Who are you?"
• Credentials validation
• Multi-factor options

**Authorization**
• Determines access rights
• "What can you do?"
• Based on policies/rules
• After authentication

**Accounting (Auditing)**
• Records activities
• "What did you do?"
• Audit trails
• Non-repudiation

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: MFA
sections.content:
**Multi-Factor Authentication:**

Must be from DIFFERENT categories!

✅ Valid MFA: Password + SMS code (know + have)
✅ Valid MFA: Fingerprint + smart card (are + have)
❌ NOT MFA: Password + security question (both "know")
❌ NOT MFA: Two fingerprints (both "are")

*Two factors from same category = single-factor*

sections.title: Access Control Models
sections.content:
**Access Control Approaches:**

**Discretionary (DAC)**
• Owner controls access
• Resource owner decides
• ACLs common
• Flexible but risky

**Mandatory (MAC)**
• System enforces access
• Labels and clearances
• Military/government
• Very restrictive

**Role-Based (RBAC)**
• Access based on job role
• Roles assigned permissions
• Users assigned roles
• Most common in business

**Attribute-Based (ABAC)**
• Access based on attributes
• User, resource, environment
• Dynamic evaluation
• Most flexible

sections.title: Access Control Principles
sections.content:
**Key Principles:**

**Least Privilege**
• Minimum access needed
• Only for required tasks
• Reduces attack surface

**Need to Know**
• Access only if required
• Even if cleared for level
• Common with MAC

**Separation of Duties**
• Divide critical functions
• No single person completes critical task
• Fraud prevention

**Defense in Depth**
• Multiple control layers
• Don't rely on single control
• Physical + logical + admin

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: AAA = Authentication (who), Authorization (what), Accounting (audit)
Auth factors: know, have, are, where, do - MFA requires different categories
Models: DAC (owner), MAC (labels), RBAC (roles), ABAC (attributes)
Principles: least privilege, need to know, separation of duties
RBAC is most common in business environments

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following BEST describes the primary purpose of the 'Accounting' component within the AAA framework?
options: Verifying a user's identity through credentials validation.
Granting access rights to resources based on pre-defined policies.
Tracking and logging user activities for auditing and accountability purposes.
Establishing secure communication channels between users and systems.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Accounting, or Auditing, focuses on tracking user activity. This includes logging access attempts, resource utilization, and other relevant events to provide an audit trail for security and compliance purposes. While authentication verifies identity and authorization grants access, accounting records what users do.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about what an auditor needs to be able to do after an event.

reveal.question: Describe the primary difference between Discretionary Access Control (DAC) and Mandatory Access Control (MAC).
reveal.answer: In DAC, the resource owner controls access to the resource. In MAC, the system enforces access control based on pre-defined labels and clearances, overriding individual user preferences.

rows.label: Access Determination
values: Access is determined by the user's assigned role.

rows.label: Flexibility
values: Highly flexible; individual users can grant or deny access.
More structured; access is managed centrally through role assignments.

rows.label: Scalability
values: Can become complex to manage in large environments.
Scales well in large organizations with well-defined roles.

title: Identity and Access Management
description: Understand IAM systems and identity lifecycle management

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: IAM is the backbone of access control. It manages who has access to what throughout their employment lifecycle. Poor IAM leads to excessive access and security gaps.

sections.title: Identity Lifecycle
sections.content:
**Identity Management Phases:**

**1. Provisioning (Joiner)**
• Create identity
• Assign initial access
• Based on role/position
• Approval workflow

**2. Maintenance (Mover)**
• Role changes
• Access modifications
• Department transfers
• Project assignments

**3. Deprovisioning (Leaver)**
• Disable account
• Remove access
• Timely execution
• Data handoff

sections.title: Provisioning Controls
sections.content:
**Provisioning Best Practices:**

**Approval Workflow**
• Manager approval
• Data owner approval
• Documented requests
• Audit trail

**Role-Based Provisioning**
• Standard access by role
• Birthright access
• Additional access on request
• Exceptions documented

**Automated Provisioning**
• Integration with HR systems
• Reduces delays
• Consistent application
• Fewer errors

**Separation of Duties**
• Requester ≠ approver
• Conflicts checked
• Compensating controls

sections.title: Access Reviews
sections.content:
**Access Certification:**

**Why Review:**
• Privilege creep
• Job changes
• Compliance requirements
• Audit preparation

**Review Types:**
• **User access review**: All user's access
• **Resource access review**: All users of a resource
• **Privileged access review**: Admin accounts
• **Role review**: Role definitions

**Frequency:**
• Privileged: Quarterly or more
• Standard: At least annually
• High-risk: More frequent

**Process:**
• Manager/owner reviews
• Approve or revoke
• Document decisions
• Track completion

sections.title: Privileged Access Management
sections.content:
**PAM Controls:**

**Credential Management**
• Vaulting (secure storage)
• Automatic rotation
• No shared passwords
• Just-in-time access

**Session Management**
• Session recording
• Keystroke logging
• Command filtering
• Time limits

**Monitoring**
• Real-time monitoring
• Alerting on anomalies
• Usage reporting
• Compliance reporting

**Key Principle:** Privileged accounts are the keys to the kingdom and require extra protection.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Identity lifecycle: provisioning, maintenance, deprovisioning
IAM components: directory, SSO, MFA, PAM, IGA
Provisioning requires approval workflow and documentation
Access reviews combat privilege creep - quarterly for privileged
PAM protects admin accounts with vaulting, monitoring, session control

title: Logical Access Controls
description: Learn about system-level access controls and security mechanisms

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Logical access controls protect digital resources at the system level. They implement the access policies defined by management through technical mechanisms.

sections.title: Operating System Security
sections.content:
**OS Access Controls:**

**User Account Management**
• Unique user IDs
• Strong password policies
• Account lockout
• Session timeout

**Access Control Lists**
• File/folder permissions
• Read, write, execute
• Owner, group, others
• Inheritance

**Security Features**
• Kernel protection
• Memory protection
• Process isolation
• Audit logging

**Hardening**
• Disable unnecessary services
• Apply patches
• Remove default accounts
• Enable logging

sections.title: Database Security
sections.content:
**Database Access Controls:**

**Authentication**
• Database accounts
• Integrated authentication
• Strong passwords
• No shared accounts

**Authorization**
• Object-level permissions
• Row-level security
• Column-level access
• Views for data hiding

**Auditing**
• Login/logout
• Failed access attempts
• Schema changes
• Data modifications

**Additional Controls**
• Encryption (TDE, column)
• Input validation
• Stored procedure security
• Backup encryption

sections.title: Application Security Controls
sections.content:
**Application-Level Access:**

**Authentication**
• Application login
• Session management
• Token handling
• SSO integration

**Authorization**
• Feature-level access
• Data-level access
• Role-based controls
• Workflow enforcement

**Session Controls**
• Session timeout
• Secure token generation
• Cookie protection
• Concurrent session limits

**Input Validation**
• Prevent injection
• Whitelisting
• Encoding output
• Parameterized queries

sections.title: Remote Access Security
sections.content:
**Remote Access Controls:**

**VPN Security**
• Strong authentication (MFA)
• Encryption (IPSec, SSL/TLS)
• Split vs. full tunneling
• Network access control

**Remote Desktop**
• MFA required
• Jump servers/bastion hosts
• Session recording
• Limited access

**Cloud Access**
• Cloud access security broker (CASB)
• Conditional access policies
• Device compliance
• User and entity behavior analytics (UEBA)

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: OS security: accounts, ACLs, security features, hardening
Password controls: length, complexity, expiration, history, lockout
Database security: authentication, authorization, auditing, encryption
Application security: session management, input validation, access control
Remote access requires MFA, encryption, and monitoring

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is the MOST effective control for preventing unauthorized access to an operating system by a user whose account has been compromised?
options: Implementing a strong password policy with regular password changes.
Enabling account lockout after a specified number of failed login attempts.
Implementing session timeout settings to automatically terminate inactive sessions.
Requiring multi-factor authentication for all user accounts.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Multi-factor authentication (MFA) provides an additional layer of security beyond a password. Even if an attacker compromises a password, they would still need the second factor (e.g., a code from a mobile device) to gain access. While the other options are important security measures, they are less effective against a compromised account than MFA.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about what adds the most layers of protection.

reveal.question: Explain the difference between object-level permissions and row-level security in database access control.
reveal.answer: Object-level permissions control access to entire database objects such as tables or views (e.g., granting SELECT access to a table). Row-level security restricts access to specific rows within a table based on user attributes or conditions. For instance, a user might only be able to see records related to their department, even if they have SELECT access to the entire table.

rows.label: Focus
values: Verifying user identity directly against the database system.
Verifying user identity through the application layer, potentially delegating to other identity providers.

rows.label: Integration
values: Tightly integrated with the database engine and its security features.
More flexible and can integrate with various authentication mechanisms like SSO or token-based systems.

rows.label: Examples
values: Database accounts with usernames and passwords, Integrated Windows Authentication.

title: Network Security Architecture
description: Understand network security design principles and technologies

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Network security controls the flow of traffic between systems and networks. Defense in depth at the network layer creates multiple barriers for attackers.

sections.title: Firewall Rules
sections.content:
**Firewall Rule Best Practices:**

**Rule Structure**
• Source address
• Destination address
• Port/protocol
• Action (allow/deny)
• Logging

**Rule Order**
• Most specific first
• Most used near top
• Deny rules before allows
• Default deny at end

**Maintenance**
• Regular rule review
• Remove unused rules
• Document rule purpose
• Change control

**Common Mistakes:**
• Overly permissive rules
• Any-any rules
• Orphaned rules
• No default deny

sections.title: IDS vs. IPS
sections.content:
**Intrusion Detection/Prevention:**

**IDS (Detection)**
• Monitors traffic
• Generates alerts
• Passive (mirror/tap)
• No traffic blocking
• Requires human response

**IPS (Prevention)**
• Monitors and blocks
• Active inline
• Real-time prevention
• Can cause disruption
• Automatic response

**Detection Methods:**
• **Signature-based**: Known attack patterns
• **Anomaly-based**: Deviation from baseline
• **Heuristic**: Behavioral analysis

**Placement:**
• Network perimeter
• Between zones
• Critical segments
• Host-based (HIDS/HIPS)

sections.title: Network Segmentation
sections.content:
**Segmentation Strategies:**

**VLANs**
• Logical separation
• Layer 2 partitioning
• ACLs between VLANs
• Reduces broadcast domain

**Zones**
• DMZ for public services
• Internal zones
• Secure zones for sensitive
• Management network

**Microsegmentation**
• Granular control
• Workload isolation
• East-west traffic control
• Zero trust approach

**Benefits:**
• Contain breaches
• Reduce attack surface
• Compliance isolation
• Traffic visibility

sections.title: Zero Trust Architecture
sections.content:
**Zero Trust Principles:**

**Core Concepts:**
• "Never trust, always verify"
• Verify explicitly
• Least privilege access
• Assume breach

**Key Components:**
• Identity verification
• Device validation
• Continuous authentication
• Microsegmentation
• Encryption everywhere

**Implementation:**
• Identity-based access
• Device health checks
• Application-level access
• No implicit trust
• Monitor all traffic

**Benefits:**
• Reduced attack surface
• Contained breaches
• Improved visibility
• Flexible architecture

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Firewall types: packet filter, stateful, proxy, NGFW, WAF
Firewall rules: specific first, default deny last
IDS detects and alerts; IPS detects and blocks
Segmentation contains breaches and reduces attack surface
Zero trust: never trust, always verify, assume breach

title: Cryptography Fundamentals
description: Master encryption concepts and cryptographic controls

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Cryptography protects data confidentiality, integrity, and authenticity. It's the foundation of secure communications, data protection, and digital trust.

sections.title: Common Algorithms
sections.content:
**Algorithm Categories:**

**Symmetric (Secret Key)**
• AES (128, 192, 256-bit) - Current standard
• 3DES - Legacy, deprecated
• DES - Obsolete, never use
• RC4 - Broken, deprecated

**Asymmetric (Public Key)**
• RSA - Digital signatures, key exchange
• ECC - Efficient, smaller keys
• Diffie-Hellman - Key exchange
• DSA - Digital signatures

**Hashing**
• SHA-256, SHA-3 - Current standards
• SHA-1 - Deprecated, avoid
• MD5 - Broken, never for security

**⚠️ CISA Tip:** Know which are weak/deprecated!

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Symmetric vs Asymmetric
sections.content:
**Symmetric = Same key** (one key, both parties)
• Fast for bulk data
• Key distribution problem
• Examples: AES, 3DES

**Asymmetric = A pair of keys** (public + private)
• Slower but solves key distribution
• Public encrypts, private decrypts
• Examples: RSA, ECC

*Use asymmetric to exchange symmetric keys, then symmetric for data!*

sections.title: Digital Signatures
sections.content:
**Digital Signature Process:**

**Signing (Sender)**
1. Hash the message
2. Encrypt hash with private key
3. Attach encrypted hash to message
4. Send message + signature

**Verification (Receiver)**
1. Decrypt signature with sender's public key
2. Hash the received message
3. Compare hashes
4. Match = authentic and unchanged

**Provides:**
• **Integrity** - Message unchanged
• **Authentication** - From claimed sender
• **Non-repudiation** - Sender can't deny

**Note:** Does NOT provide confidentiality (use encryption for that)

sections.title: PKI Components
sections.content:
**Public Key Infrastructure:**

**Certificate Authority (CA)**
• Issues certificates
• Validates identity
• Maintains CRL
• Revokes certificates

**Registration Authority (RA)**
• Verifies identity
• Forwards to CA
• Administrative role

**Certificate**
• Public key
• Owner identity
• CA signature
• Validity period

**Trust Model:**
• Root CA (self-signed)
• Subordinate CAs
• End-entity certificates
• Certificate chain

sections.title: Key Management
sections.content:
**Key Lifecycle:**

**Generation**
• Sufficient key length
• Secure random generation
• Secure environment

**Distribution**
• Secure key exchange
• Out-of-band verification
• Key encryption keys

**Storage**
• Hardware security modules (HSM)
• Key vaults
• Encryption of keys
• Access control

**Rotation**
• Regular replacement
• Crypto-period limits
• Automated where possible

**Destruction**
• Secure deletion
• All copies
• Audit trail

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Symmetric = same key (fast); Asymmetric = key pair (solves distribution)
Current standards: AES (symmetric), RSA/ECC (asymmetric), SHA-256 (hash)
Digital signatures provide integrity, authentication, non-repudiation
PKI uses CAs to issue and manage certificates
Key management covers generation, distribution, storage, rotation, destruction

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following symmetric encryption algorithms is MOST appropriate for a new system requiring strong security and long-term viability?
options: DES
3DES
AES (256-bit)
RC4
knowledgeCheck.explanation: AES with a 256-bit key is the current standard and provides the strongest security among the options. DES and 3DES are legacy algorithms and are vulnerable to attacks. RC4 is broken and should never be used.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the current industry standards and the strength of the key length.

reveal.question: What is the FIRST step a sender takes when creating a digital signature for a message?
reveal.answer: The sender first calculates a hash of the message. This hash acts as a unique fingerprint of the message and is then encrypted with the sender's private key.

rows.label: Key Distribution
values: Public key can be openly distributed

title: Internet and Web Security
description: Understand web application security and internet threats

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The internet is the primary attack vector for most organizations. Web applications and email are the most common entry points for attackers.

sections.title: OWASP Top 10 Overview
sections.content:
**Key Web Vulnerabilities:**

**Injection**
• SQL, OS, LDAP injection
• Untrusted data as commands
• Parameterized queries prevent

**Broken Authentication**
• Weak credentials
• Session fixation
• Credential stuffing

**Sensitive Data Exposure**
• Unencrypted data
• Weak crypto
• Missing TLS

**XML External Entities (XXE)**
• XML parser attacks
• File disclosure
• Disable external entities

**Broken Access Control**
• Missing authorization checks
• IDOR (Insecure Direct Object Reference)
• Enforce access control server-side

sections.title: Email Security
sections.content:
**Email Protection:**

**Spam/Phishing Protection**
• Spam filters
• Phishing detection
• Link analysis
• Sandboxing attachments

**Email Authentication**
• **SPF** - Sender Policy Framework (authorized senders)
• **DKIM** - DomainKeys (email signing)
• **DMARC** - Domain-based authentication

**Encryption**
• TLS for transport
• S/MIME for message encryption
• PGP for end-to-end

**DLP Integration**
• Content inspection
• Sensitive data detection
• Policy enforcement

sections.title: Secure Protocols
sections.content:
**Protocol Security:**

**Secure Alternatives:**
• HTTP → HTTPS (TLS)
• FTP → SFTP or FTPS
• Telnet → SSH
• SMTP → SMTP with TLS
• DNS → DNS over HTTPS (DoH)

**TLS Best Practices:**
• TLS 1.2 minimum (1.3 preferred)
• Strong cipher suites
• Disable weak algorithms
• Certificate validation

**VPN Protocols:**
• IPSec - Network layer
• SSL/TLS VPN - Application layer
• WireGuard - Modern, fast
• Deprecated: PPTP (never use)

sections.title: Web Security Controls
sections.content:
**Web Protection Mechanisms:**

**WAF (Web Application Firewall)**
• HTTP/HTTPS inspection
• Attack signature blocking
• Virtual patching
• Rate limiting

**Secure Headers**
• Content-Security-Policy (CSP)
• HTTP Strict Transport Security (HSTS)
• X-Frame-Options
• X-Content-Type-Options

**Bot Protection**
• CAPTCHA
• Rate limiting
• Behavioral analysis
• IP reputation

**CDN Security**
• DDoS protection
• Origin hiding
• Edge caching
• SSL termination

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: OWASP Top 10: injection, broken auth, XSS, sensitive data exposure, etc.
Email security: SPF, DKIM, DMARC for authentication; TLS/S/MIME for encryption
Use secure protocol versions: HTTPS, SFTP, SSH, TLS 1.2+
WAF provides application-layer protection for web apps
Security headers (CSP, HSTS) provide browser-enforced protection

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is the MOST effective method for preventing SQL injection vulnerabilities?
options: Implementing strong password policies for database users.
Regularly patching the operating system of the database server.
Using parameterized queries (prepared statements) when interacting with the database.
Enabling verbose error logging on the database server to identify potential attacks.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Parameterized queries (prepared statements) treat user input as data, not as executable code, thus preventing SQL injection attacks. Strong passwords and OS patching are important security measures but do not directly address SQL injection. Verbose error logging can help in identifying attacks after they occur, but does not prevent them.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about how the database interprets user-supplied input.

reveal.question: Explain the purpose of SPF, DKIM, and DMARC in email security.
reveal.answer: SPF (Sender Policy Framework) verifies that emails are sent from authorized mail servers for a domain. DKIM (DomainKeys Identified Mail) provides a digital signature to emails, verifying the email's authenticity and integrity. DMARC (Domain-based Message Authentication, Reporting & Conformance) builds on SPF and DKIM to define policies for handling emails that fail authentication checks and provides reporting mechanisms to domain owners.

rows.label: HTTP
values: HTTP transmits data in plaintext, making it vulnerable to eavesdropping and man-in-the-middle attacks. HTTPS encrypts the communication using TLS.

rows.label: FTP
values: FTP transmits data and credentials in plaintext. SFTP uses SSH for secure file transfer, while FTPS adds TLS encryption to FTP.

rows.label: Telnet
values: Telnet transmits data in plaintext, including usernames and passwords. SSH encrypts the entire session, preventing eavesdropping.

rows.label: SMTP
values: Standard SMTP sends emails without encryption. SMTP with TLS encrypts the email transmission, protecting sensitive information during transit.

title: Physical and Environmental Security
description: Learn physical security controls for protecting information assets

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Physical security is the first line of defense. The best cybersecurity is worthless if an attacker can physically access your systems.

sections.title: Access Control Methods
sections.content:
**Physical Access Controls:**

**Mechanical**
• Locks and keys
• Combination locks
• Master key systems

**Electronic**
• Badge/card access
• PIN codes
• Biometric (fingerprint, retina, face)
• Smart cards

**Procedural**
• Visitor logs
• Escort requirements
• Sign-in/sign-out
• Tailgating prevention

**Mantrap/Airlock**
• Two-door system
• One open at a time
• Forces authentication
• Prevents tailgating

sections.title: Surveillance Systems
sections.content:
**Surveillance Components:**

**CCTV**
• Monitor entry/exit
• Record for evidence
• Deter behaviors
• Integration with access control

**Camera Types**
• Fixed vs. PTZ (pan-tilt-zoom)
• IP vs. analog
• Indoor vs. outdoor
• Visible vs. covert

**Recording**
• Retention period
• Storage requirements
• Secure access
• Chain of custody

**Monitoring**
• Real-time viewing
• Motion detection
• Analytics
• Guard station

sections.title: Environmental Threats
sections.content:
**Environmental Risks:**

**Fire**
• Detection systems
• Suppression (clean agent, water)
• Prevention (no combustibles)
• Evacuation procedures

**Water**
• Flood risk assessment
• Water detection sensors
• Raised floors
• Equipment placement

**Climate**
• Temperature control (64-75°F / 18-24°C)
• Humidity control (40-60%)
• HVAC redundancy
• Hot/cold aisle containment

**Power**
• UPS (short-term)
• Generator (long-term)
• Surge protection
• Redundant feeds

sections.title: Mobile Device Physical Security
sections.content:
**Protecting Mobile Assets:**

**Laptops**
• Cable locks
• Encrypted drives
• Asset tracking
• Remote wipe capability

**Phones/Tablets**
• MDM enrollment
• Screen lock required
• Encryption
• Find/remote wipe

**Media**
• Secure storage
• Encryption
• Proper disposal
• Chain of custody

**Travel Security**
• Never leave unattended
• Hotel safe usage
• Customs considerations
• VPN usage on public WiFi

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Physical security uses defense in depth with multiple layers
Access controls: mechanical, electronic, procedural
Surveillance provides deterrence, detection, and evidence
Environmental controls: fire, water, climate, power
Mobile devices need encryption, MDM, and remote wipe

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following access control methods is MOST effective in preventing tailgating in a high-security data center environment?
options: A. Installing CCTV cameras focused on the entrance.
B. Implementing a mandatory sign-in/sign-out sheet for all personnel.
C. Deploying turnstiles with biometric authentication.
D. Requiring all visitors to be escorted by authorized employees.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Turnstiles with biometric authentication provide the strongest deterrent against tailgating. They physically prevent more than one person from entering at a time and verify identity before granting access. While the other options are helpful, they are less effective at physically preventing tailgating.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the physical limitations of each method in preventing multiple individuals from entering on a single authentication.

reveal.question: What is the generally recommended temperature range (in both Fahrenheit and Celsius) for optimal environmental control in a data center?
reveal.answer: The recommended temperature range is 64-75°F (18-24°C). This range helps prevent equipment overheating and ensures stable operation.

rows.label: Flexibility
values: Dynamic, can be remotely controlled to pan, tilt, and zoom

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following fire suppression systems is MOST suitable for a server room containing sensitive electronic equipment?
options: A. Water sprinkler system
B. Dry chemical extinguisher
C. Clean agent suppression system
D. Carbon dioxide (CO2) flooding system
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Clean agent suppression systems are designed to extinguish fires without damaging electronic equipment. They are non-conductive and leave no residue, unlike water or dry chemical extinguishers. While CO2 is also non-damaging, it presents an asphyxiation risk to personnel.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the potential damage to electronic equipment when choosing a fire suppression system.

title: Security Awareness and Training
description: Understand security awareness program design and delivery

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: People are often the weakest link in security. Awareness training transforms employees from security liabilities into security assets. Technical controls alone cannot stop social engineering.

sections.title: Awareness Program Elements
sections.content:
**Effective Awareness Programs:**

**Content Topics:**
• Password security
• Phishing recognition
• Data handling
• Physical security
• Reporting procedures
• Social media risks

**Delivery Methods:**
• Online modules
• Live presentations
• Newsletters/emails
• Posters and screensavers
• Gamification
• Phishing simulations

**Measurement:**
• Completion rates
• Assessment scores
• Phishing test results
• Incident trends
• Reported suspicious activity

sections.title: Social Engineering Threats
sections.content:
**Social Engineering Techniques:**

**Phishing**
• Email-based deception
• Fake login pages
• Malicious attachments
• Urgent calls to action

**Spear Phishing**
• Targeted at individuals
• Personalized content
• Higher success rate
• Research-based

**Vishing**
• Voice phishing
• Phone-based attacks
• Impersonation
• Urgency tactics

**Pretexting**
• Fabricated scenario
• Builds trust over time
• Information gathering
• Authority impersonation

**Baiting**
• Enticing offers
• Infected USB drives
• Malicious downloads

sections.title: Building Security Culture
sections.content:
**Culture Components:**

**Leadership**
• Executive sponsorship
• Visible commitment
• Budget allocation
• Tone from top

**Communication**
• Regular messaging
• Clear expectations
• Open reporting
• No blame culture

**Reinforcement**
• Recognition programs
• Positive feedback
• Consistent enforcement
• Continuous improvement

**Integration**
• Part of onboarding
• Ongoing refresher
• Performance objectives
• Business alignment

sections.title: Training for Technical Roles
sections.content:
**Role-Specific Training:**

**Developers**
• Secure coding practices
• OWASP Top 10
• Security testing
• Code review

**System Administrators**
• Hardening procedures
• Patch management
• Incident response
• Monitoring

**Help Desk**
• Social engineering recognition
• Verification procedures
• Escalation protocols
• Documentation

**Executives**
• Risk management
• Compliance requirements
• Incident handling
• Business email compromise

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Awareness builds understanding; training builds skills
Effective programs use multiple delivery methods
Social engineering attacks people, not technology
Security culture requires leadership commitment
Role-specific training addresses unique risks per job function

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following social engineering techniques is MOST likely to involve personalized content and extensive prior research on the target?
options: Phishing
Spear Phishing
Vishing
Smishing
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Spear phishing is a targeted attack that relies on personalized content and research to increase its success rate. Phishing is more generic, while vishing uses voice communication, and smishing uses SMS.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider which technique requires the attacker to know specific details about the victim.

reveal.question: List three key components of building a strong security culture within an organization.
reveal.answer: Leadership: Executive sponsorship, visible commitment, budget allocation, tone from the top. Communication: Regular messaging, clear expectations, open reporting, no-blame culture. Reinforcement: Recognition programs, positive feedback, consistent enforcement.

title: Malware and Endpoint Security
description: Understand malware types and endpoint protection strategies

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Malware is a primary attack vector. Endpoints are where users work and where data lives. Protecting endpoints is essential for overall security.

sections.title: Malware Behavior
sections.content:
**How Malware Works:**

**Initial Access**
• Phishing emails
• Malicious downloads
• Infected USB drives
• Exploit kits

**Execution**
• User runs file
• Exploit vulnerability
• Script execution
• Macro activation

**Persistence**
• Registry modifications
• Scheduled tasks
• Startup programs
• Boot sector infection

**Actions**
• Data theft
• Encryption (ransomware)
• Botnet enrollment
• Lateral movement
• Cryptocurrency mining

sections.title: Antivirus Approaches
sections.content:
**Detection Methods:**

**Signature-Based**
• Known malware patterns
• Fast detection
• Requires updates
• Misses zero-day

**Heuristic**
• Detect suspicious behavior
• Catches variants
• False positives possible
• More resource intensive

**Behavioral**
• Monitors execution
• Detects actual malice
• Real-time protection
• Can stop unknown threats

**Machine Learning**
• Pattern recognition
• Adapts over time
• Reduces false positives
• Cloud-powered

sections.title: Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR)
sections.content:
**EDR Capabilities:**

**Detection**
• Real-time monitoring
• Behavioral analysis
• Threat correlation
• Indicator matching

**Response**
• Isolate endpoint
• Terminate processes
• Quarantine files
• Collect forensic data

**Investigation**
• Process trees
• Timeline analysis
• Network connections
• File modifications

**Hunting**
• Proactive threat search
• IOC queries
• Pattern analysis
• Historical data search

sections.title: Endpoint Protection Best Practices
sections.content:
**Endpoint Security Controls:**

**Prevention**
• Updated antivirus/EDR
• Application whitelisting
• Least privilege
• Host-based firewall

**Detection**
• Real-time monitoring
• Behavioral analytics
• Log collection
• Alerting

**Response**
• Incident response plan
• Isolation capability
• Remediation procedures
• Evidence collection

**Maintenance**
• Patch management
• Configuration hardening
• Regular scanning
• Policy updates

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Malware types: virus, worm, trojan, ransomware, spyware, rootkit
AV methods: signature, heuristic, behavioral, machine learning
EDR adds detection, response, and investigation capabilities
Defense in depth: prevention, detection, response, maintenance
Behavioral detection catches unknown (zero-day) threats

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following initial access methods for malware is MOST likely to bypass traditional signature-based antivirus solutions?
options: A user downloading and executing a file from a well-known, blacklisted domain.
A phishing email containing a macro-enabled document targeting a zero-day vulnerability.
An infected USB drive containing a common virus signature already present in the antivirus database.
A malicious download from a website known to distribute malware, detected by a URL filter.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A zero-day vulnerability, by definition, is unknown to security vendors and therefore not covered by signature-based detection. The other options involve known threats that are likely to be detected by traditional methods.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about what makes a method 'new' or 'unknown' to security systems.

reveal.question: List three key capabilities of an EDR system.
reveal.answer:
1. Real-time Monitoring: Continuously observing endpoint activity for suspicious behavior.
2. Threat Response: Providing tools to isolate endpoints, terminate malicious processes, and quarantine files.
3. Investigation: Offering features like process trees and timeline analysis to understand the scope and impact of an incident.

rows.label: Detection Basis
values: Actions and impact of running code

title: Security Monitoring and Logging
description: Master security event management and monitoring practices

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: You can't protect what you can't see. Security monitoring provides visibility into threats and attacks. Effective logging is essential for detection and forensics.

sections.title: Log Management
sections.content:
**Log Management Process:**

**Collection**
• Identify log sources
• Configure logging
• Secure transport
• Normalize format

**Storage**
• Centralized repository
• Secure storage
• Retention policies
• Archive management

**Protection**
• Integrity verification
• Access controls
• Encryption
• Tampering prevention

**Analysis**
• Search and query
• Correlation
• Alerting
• Reporting

sections.title: SIEM Overview
sections.content:
**Security Information and Event Management:**

**Core Functions:**
• Log aggregation
• Event correlation
• Alerting
• Dashboards
• Reporting
• Incident workflow

**Correlation Use Cases:**
• Brute force detection
• Privilege escalation
• Lateral movement
• Data exfiltration
• Compliance monitoring

**Benefits:**
• Centralized visibility
• Real-time detection
• Historical analysis
• Compliance reporting
• Incident investigation

sections.title: Monitoring Best Practices
sections.content:
**Effective Monitoring:**

**What to Monitor:**
• Authentication events
• Privileged activity
• Changes to security settings
• Network anomalies
• Data access patterns

**Alert Management:**
• Tune to reduce false positives
• Prioritize by risk
• Define response procedures
• Escalation paths

**Operations:**
• 24/7 coverage for critical
• Defined SLAs
• Trained analysts
• Documented runbooks

**Continuous Improvement:**
• Regular rule tuning
• New threat indicators
• Lessons learned
• Red team findings

sections.title: Threat Intelligence
sections.content:
**Using Threat Intelligence:**

**Types:**
• **Strategic**: High-level trends, planning
• **Tactical**: TTPs, attack methods
• **Operational**: Campaign details
• **Technical**: IOCs, signatures

**Sources:**
• Commercial feeds
• Open source (OSINT)
• ISACs (industry sharing)
• Government (CISA, FBI)
• Internal research

**Integration:**
• SIEM correlation
• Firewall/IPS rules
• Email security
• Endpoint detection

**IOC Types:**
• IP addresses
• Domains/URLs
• File hashes
• Email addresses
• Behavioral patterns

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Log management: collect, store, protect, analyze
SIEM provides aggregation, correlation, alerting, and reporting
Monitor authentication, privileged activity, changes, anomalies
Threat intelligence: strategic, tactical, operational, technical
Continuous tuning reduces false positives and improves detection

sections.title: SIEM and Log Management: Understanding the Difference

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following BEST describes the primary difference between log management and a Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) system?
options: Log management focuses on the collection, storage, and protection of log data, while a SIEM uses that data for correlation, alerting, and incident response.
SIEM systems are primarily concerned with long-term archival of log data, whereas log management handles real-time analysis.
Log management is a component of a SIEM system, focusing solely on reporting capabilities.
SIEM systems handle only network security logs, while log management encompasses all system and application logs.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Log management is the foundation for collecting, storing, and protecting log data. A SIEM builds upon this by analyzing the logs, correlating events, generating alerts, and facilitating incident response. The SIEM adds intelligence and context that basic log management lacks.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about which system *uses* the other's output.

reveal.question: What are the four core phases of the Log Management Process?
reveal.answer: The four core phases of the Log Management Process are: Collection, Storage, Protection, and Operation (as mentioned in the 'Monitoring Best Practices' section, which assumes logs are being actively used). Each phase has sub-elements. Collection includes identifying log sources, configuring logging, securing transport, and normalizing format. Storage includes a centralized repository, secure storage, retention policies, and archive management. Protection includes integrity verification, access controls, and encryption.

rows.label: Goal
values: Focus on the most critical threats.

rows.label: Method
values: Tune alert thresholds and correlation rules.
Assign severity levels based on potential impact and likelihood.

rows.label: Importance
values: Reduces alert fatigue and improves efficiency.
Ensures timely response to the most damaging incidents.

title: Cloud Security
description: Understand cloud security controls and shared responsibility

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Cloud computing changes security responsibilities. Understanding what the provider secures versus what you secure is critical for avoiding gaps.

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Cloud Models
sections.content:
**IaaS = Infrastructure** - You manage OS up
Example: AWS EC2, Azure VMs

**PaaS = Platform** - Provider manages through runtime
Example: Azure App Service, Heroku

**SaaS = Software** - Provider manages everything
Example: Salesforce, Office 365

*More "aaS" = Less you manage = More provider responsibility*

sections.title: Cloud Security Controls
sections.content:
**Key Cloud Controls:**

**Identity and Access**
• IAM policies
• MFA enforcement
• Least privilege
• Federated identity

**Network Security**
• VPC/virtual networks
• Security groups
• Network ACLs
• Private endpoints

**Data Protection**
• Encryption at rest/transit
• Key management
• Data classification
• DLP

**Monitoring**
• Cloud-native logging
• SIEM integration
• Alerts and dashboards
• Compliance reporting

sections.title: Cloud Governance
sections.content:
**Governing Cloud Use:**

**Policy Framework**
• Approved providers
• Approved services
• Classification requirements
• Security standards

**Risk Assessment**
• Vendor due diligence
• Data location concerns
• Compliance requirements
• Exit strategy

**Monitoring**
• Shadow IT detection
• Usage monitoring
• Cost management
• Compliance verification

**Vendor Management**
• Contract terms
• SLAs
• Right to audit
• Subprocessor management

sections.title: CASB (Cloud Access Security Broker)
sections.content:
**CASB Functions:**

**Visibility**
• Shadow IT discovery
• Usage analytics
• Risk scoring
• User behavior

**Compliance**
• DLP enforcement
• Encryption
• Access controls
• Regulatory compliance

**Threat Protection**
• Malware detection
• Anomaly detection
• Data exfiltration prevention
• Account compromise

**Deployment:**
• API-based (out-of-band)
• Proxy-based (inline)
• Agent-based (endpoint)

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Shared responsibility varies by model (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS)
Customer always responsible for data classification
Cloud controls: IAM, network, data protection, monitoring
Governance includes policy, risk assessment, monitoring
CASB provides visibility, compliance, and threat protection for cloud

knowledgeCheck.question: An organization is migrating sensitive customer data to a public cloud provider. Which of the following controls is MOST critical to implement to protect the confidentiality of the data both in transit and at rest?
options: Implementing multi-factor authentication (MFA) for all user accounts.
Implementing encryption for data at rest and in transit using robust key management practices.
Configuring network security groups to restrict access to the virtual network.
Establishing a formal incident response plan with the cloud provider.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Encryption is paramount for data confidentiality, especially when data resides with a third-party cloud provider. Robust key management is essential to ensure the keys themselves are protected. MFA and network security are important, but encryption directly addresses data confidentiality. Incident response is crucial, but less impactful to data confidentiality than encryption.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the core principle of protecting sensitive data: preventing unauthorized access even if other controls fail.

reveal.question: What are four key elements that should be included in a cloud governance policy framework?
reveal.answer: The cloud governance policy framework should address: Approved Providers, Approved Services, Classification Requirements, and Security Standards. These elements guide cloud usage and ensure alignment with organizational policies and compliance requirements.

rows.label: Rule Processing
values: Rules are processed in order; first matching rule wins

knowledgeCheck.question: When performing vendor due diligence for a cloud provider, which of the following is the MOST important consideration for an IS auditor?
options: The cloud provider's marketing materials and customer testimonials.
The cloud provider's SOC 2 Type II report and security certifications.
The cloud provider's pricing structure and service level agreements (SLAs).
The cloud provider's physical security controls for their data centers.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: SOC 2 Type II reports and security certifications provide independent assurance of the cloud provider's security controls and compliance with industry standards. Marketing materials are biased, pricing is a business decision, and while physical security is important, it's less critical than understanding the provider's broader security posture.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about what provides independent verification of a vendor's security practices.

title: Privacy and Data Protection
description: Understand privacy principles and data protection regulations

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Privacy regulations are expanding globally with significant penalties. Organizations must protect personal data not only for compliance but for customer trust.

sections.title: Privacy Principles
sections.content:
**Core Privacy Principles:**

**Collection Limitation**
• Collect only what's needed
• Lawful and fair means
• With knowledge/consent

**Purpose Specification**
• Stated purpose at collection
• No use beyond purpose

**Use Limitation**
• Use only for stated purpose
• Unless consent or law

**Data Quality**
• Accurate and complete
• Current as needed

**Security Safeguards**
• Protect against loss/misuse
• Appropriate controls

**Openness**
• Transparent practices
• Available policies

**Individual Participation**
• Access to own data
• Ability to correct

**Accountability**
• Organization responsible
• Demonstrable compliance

sections.title: GDPR Key Requirements
sections.content:
**GDPR Fundamentals:**

**Lawful Basis for Processing**
• Consent
• Contract
• Legal obligation
• Vital interests
• Public task
• Legitimate interests

**Data Subject Rights**
• Access
• Rectification
• Erasure ("Right to be forgotten")
• Portability
• Object to processing

**Organization Requirements**
• Data protection officer (if required)
• Privacy impact assessments
• Breach notification (72 hours)
• Records of processing
• Privacy by design

**Penalties:**
• Up to 4% annual global revenue or €20M

sections.title: Privacy by Design
sections.content:
**PbD Principles:**

**1. Proactive not Reactive**
• Prevent privacy issues
• Not just respond to them

**2. Default Privacy**
• Maximum privacy by default
• No action required by user

**3. Embedded in Design**
• Built into systems
• Not bolted on after

**4. Full Functionality**
• Privacy AND functionality
• Not zero-sum tradeoff

**5. End-to-End Security**
• Cradle to grave protection
• Throughout data lifecycle

**6. Visibility and Transparency**
• Open practices
• Subject to verification

**7. User-Centric**
• Respect user privacy
• User-friendly options

sections.title: Privacy Impact Assessment
sections.content:
**PIA Process:**

**When Required:**
• New processing activities
• High-risk processing
• New technology
• Large-scale processing

**Assessment Elements:**
• Description of processing
• Purpose and necessity
• Risks to individuals
• Mitigation measures
• Stakeholder input

**Outcomes:**
• Risk acceptance
• Risk mitigation
• Process changes
• Controls implementation
• Consultation with DPA

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Privacy principles: collection/use limitation, security, accountability
GDPR requires lawful basis, data subject rights, breach notification
Privacy by Design embeds privacy into systems proactively
PIA assesses privacy risks of new processing activities
Penalties for violations can be significant (up to 4% revenue)

knowledgeCheck.question: An organization is processing personal data based on 'legitimate interests.' Under GDPR, which of the following actions is MOST critical to demonstrate compliance?
options: A. Obtaining explicit consent from all data subjects.
B. Conducting a Legitimate Interests Assessment (LIA) and documenting it thoroughly.
C. Appointing a Data Protection Officer (DPO).
D. Implementing Privacy by Design principles.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: While all options are important aspects of GDPR compliance, when relying on 'legitimate interests' as the lawful basis for processing, a Legitimate Interests Assessment (LIA) is paramount. It demonstrates that the organization has carefully considered the balance between its interests and the data subjects' rights and freedoms. Consent is not required when legitimate interest is the basis. A DPO is only required in specific circumstances. Privacy by Design is a general principle, not specific to legitimate interests.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the specific requirements when using 'legitimate interests' as the lawful basis for processing.

reveal.question: List the seven founding principles of Privacy by Design.
reveal.answer:
1. Proactive not Reactive; Preventative not Remedial.
2. Privacy as the Default Setting.
3. Privacy Embedded into Design.
4. Full Functionality – Positive-Sum, not Zero-Sum.
5. End-to-End Security – Full Lifecycle Protection.
6. Visibility and Transparency – Keep it Open.
7. Respect for User Privacy – Keep it User-Centric.

rows.label: Definition
values: Freely given, specific, informed and unambiguous indication of the data subject's wishes.

Processing is necessary for the legitimate interests pursued by the controller or by a third party, except where such interests are overridden by the interests or fundamental rights and freedoms of the data subject.

rows.label: Data Subject Control
values: Data subject has direct and explicit control; can withdraw consent at any time.
Data subject has the right to object to processing based on legitimate interests.

rows.label: Documentation
values: Requires clear record of consent obtained.
Requires a documented Legitimate Interests Assessment (LIA).

title: AI and Machine Learning Security
description: Security considerations for artificial intelligence and machine learning systems

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: AI/ML systems are increasingly integrated into business-critical applications. Understanding their unique security risks is essential for auditors as these systems can make decisions affecting customers, operations, and compliance.

sections.title: AI/ML Security Risks
sections.content:
**Key Risk Categories:**

**Data Risks**
• Training data poisoning
• Data leakage from models
• Bias in training data
• Privacy violations
• Inadequate data provenance

**Model Risks**
• Model theft/extraction
• Adversarial attacks
• Model drift and degradation
• Unexplainable decisions
• Overfitting vulnerabilities

**Operational Risks**
• Hallucinations/fabrications
• Prompt injection attacks
• Unauthorized model access
• Lack of human oversight
• Integration vulnerabilities

sections.title: AI Security Controls
rows: Data lineage, quality validation, bias testing

sections.title: Adversarial Attacks on AI
sections.content:
**Common Attack Types:**

**Evasion Attacks**
• Manipulating inputs to cause misclassification
• Adding noise imperceptible to humans
• Bypassing detection systems

**Poisoning Attacks**
• Corrupting training data
• Injecting malicious samples
• Creating backdoors in models

**Model Extraction**
• Querying to reverse-engineer model
• Stealing intellectual property
• Creating adversarial examples

**Inference Attacks**
• Membership inference
• Attribute inference
• Model inversion to reveal training data

sections.title: AI Governance Framework
sections.content:
**Governance Elements:**

**Ethics and Oversight**
• AI ethics committee
• Use case approval process
• Human-in-the-loop requirements
• Bias assessment

**Risk Management**
• AI-specific risk assessment
• Impact classification
• Third-party AI evaluation
• Incident response for AI failures

**Compliance**
• Regulatory requirements (EU AI Act)
• Industry standards
• Documentation requirements
• Audit trail maintenance

**Lifecycle Management**
• Model inventory
• Version control
• Retirement procedures
• Continuous monitoring

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: AI Security DAMP
sections.content:
**D-A-M-P:**
• **D**ata integrity - Protect training data
• **A**dversarial defense - Resist attacks
• **M**odel governance - Control lifecycle
• **P**rivacy protection - Prevent data leakage

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: AI systems face unique risks: data poisoning, adversarial attacks, model theft
Adversarial attacks include evasion, poisoning, extraction, and inference
AI governance includes ethics oversight, risk management, and compliance
Explainability and audit trails are critical for AI accountability
Auditors should assess training data quality, model controls, and monitoring

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following BEST describes the primary goal of a 'poisoning attack' on an AI/ML system?
options: To manipulate input data at inference time to cause misclassification.
To corrupt the training data used to build the model, introducing vulnerabilities.
To extract the model's parameters and architecture for malicious purposes.
To overload the AI system's processing capacity, causing a denial-of-service.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Poisoning attacks directly target the training data, injecting malicious samples to create backdoors or degrade the model's overall performance. This contrasts with evasion attacks, which target the model during inference.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider which attack type impacts the model's training phase.

reveal.question: What are the three core governance elements?
reveal.answer: The three core governance elements are Ethics and Oversight, Risk Management, and Compliance.

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is the MOST appropriate action for an AI Ethics Committee to take when a proposed AI application is found to have a high risk of perpetuating existing societal biases?
options: Approve the application with a disclaimer about potential biases.
Implement continuous monitoring of the application's outputs for biased results.
Reject the application and require modifications to address the bias issue.
Proceed with the application, but allocate additional resources for post-implementation bias mitigation.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The primary responsibility of an AI Ethics Committee is to prevent the deployment of AI systems that could cause harm or perpetuate biases. Rejecting the application and requiring modifications is the most proactive and ethical approach.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the proactive vs. reactive approach to ethical concerns.

title: Advanced Cloud Security
description: Deep dive into cloud security architecture, controls, and audit considerations

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Cloud adoption continues accelerating. Auditors must understand cloud-native security concepts, shared responsibility nuances, and emerging cloud security challenges like container security and serverless computing.

sections.title: Cloud Security Posture Management (CSPM)
sections.content:
**CSPM Capabilities:**

**Configuration Monitoring**
• Continuous compliance checks
• Drift detection
• Misconfiguration alerts
• CIS benchmark validation

**Risk Assessment**
• Asset inventory
• Vulnerability prioritization
• Attack path analysis
• Risk scoring

**Remediation**
• Automated fixes
• Policy enforcement
• Infrastructure as Code scanning
• Guardrails implementation

**Key Audit Points:**
• CSPM tool coverage
• Alert response procedures
• Remediation timelines
• Policy exception handling

sections.title: Container Security
rows: Vulnerability scanning, signed images, minimal base

sections.title: Kubernetes Security
sections.content:
**K8s Security Domains:**

**Authentication & Authorization**
• Service accounts
• RBAC policies
• Pod security admission
• Network policies

**Secrets Management**
• Encrypted secrets
• External secrets operators
• Vault integration
• Rotation policies

**Runtime Security**
• Pod security standards
• Runtime threat detection
• Audit logging
• Admission controllers

**Supply Chain**
• Image signing (Sigstore)
• SBOM generation
• Policy enforcement
• Provenance verification

sections.title: Serverless Security
sections.content:
**Serverless Security Considerations:**

**Function Security**
• Minimal permissions (least privilege)
• Environment variable protection
• Dependency vulnerabilities
• Code injection prevention

**Configuration**
• Timeout settings
• Memory limits
• Concurrency controls
• VPC integration

**Monitoring**
• Invocation logging
• Error tracking
• Performance monitoring
• Security event detection

**Challenges:**
• Limited visibility
• Ephemeral nature
• Complex event triggers
• Third-party dependencies

sections.title: Multi-Cloud Considerations
sections.content:
**Multi-Cloud Security:**

**Governance**
• Consistent security policies
• Centralized identity management
• Cross-cloud visibility
• Unified compliance reporting

**Technical Controls**
• Cloud-agnostic security tools
• Consistent encryption standards
• Unified logging and monitoring
• Cross-cloud network security

**Challenges:**
• Skill gaps across platforms
• Tool sprawl
• Inconsistent native controls
• Complex incident response

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: CSPM provides continuous cloud configuration monitoring and compliance
Container security spans image, registry, orchestration, runtime, and host layers
Kubernetes requires RBAC, network policies, secrets management, and pod security
Serverless security focuses on minimal permissions and dependency management
Multi-cloud requires unified governance, identity, and monitoring approaches

knowledgeCheck.question: An organization is implementing a CSPM solution to enhance its cloud security. Which of the following CSPM capabilities would be MOST effective in identifying deviations from established security standards and policies?
options: Vulnerability Prioritization
Configuration Monitoring
Attack Path Analysis
Asset Inventory
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Configuration Monitoring directly addresses deviations from established security standards through continuous compliance checks, drift detection, and misconfiguration alerts. The other options are important but don't directly focus on identifying deviations from existing policies.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about which capability is focused on the *configuration* of cloud resources.

reveal.question: List the three primary security domains within a Kubernetes environment.
reveal.answer:
1. Authentication & Authorization
2. Secrets Management
3. Runtime Security

rows.label: Primary Focus
values: Identifying and categorizing all cloud resources.
Determining potential routes attackers could take to compromise critical assets.

rows.label: Outcome
values: Comprehensive list of assets for security management.
Prioritized list of vulnerabilities based on their exploitability and impact.

knowledgeCheck.question: When securing serverless functions, which of the following is the MOST critical consideration regarding permissions?
options: Granting functions broad access to all cloud resources for ease of development.
Ensuring functions have the minimal permissions necessary to perform their intended tasks.
Using a single service account for all functions to simplify management.
Disabling all network access to minimize the attack surface.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The principle of least privilege is paramount in serverless security. Granting minimal permissions limits the potential damage if a function is compromised. Broad access, shared service accounts, and complete network isolation are not best practices.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the principle of 'least privilege'.

title: IoT and OT Security
description: Security for Internet of Things and Operational Technology environments

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: IoT and OT systems control physical processes and critical infrastructure. Security failures can cause physical harm, production disruption, and safety incidents. Auditors must understand the unique challenges of these environments.

sections.title: IoT Security Challenges
sections.content:
**Key IoT Challenges:**

**Device Constraints**
• Limited processing power
• Memory constraints
• Battery limitations
• Difficult to update

**Security Gaps**
• Default credentials
• Unencrypted communications
• Lack of authentication
• No security updates

**Management Challenges**
• Device inventory
• Patch management
• Lifecycle management
• Shadow IoT

**Network Risks**
• Large attack surface
• Lateral movement
• Botnets (e.g., Mirai)
• DDoS participation

sections.title: OT/ICS Security Framework
sections.content:
**ICS Security Layers (Purdue Model):**

**Level 0-1: Field Devices**
• Sensors, actuators, PLCs
• Physical security critical
• Limited cyber controls

**Level 2: Control Systems**
• HMI, engineering workstations
• Network segmentation
• Application whitelisting

**Level 3: Operations**
• Historians, OT servers
• Jump servers for access
• Logging and monitoring

**Level 3.5: DMZ**
• Data diodes
• Secure file transfer
• Patch management

**Level 4-5: Enterprise**
• Standard IT controls
• Identity management
• Security operations

sections.title: IT/OT Convergence Security
sections.content:
**Convergence Challenges:**

**Cultural**
• Different priorities (CIA vs AIC)
• Different skill sets
• Different risk tolerances
• Communication gaps

**Technical**
• Legacy protocol security
• Incompatible security tools
• Real-time requirements
• Vendor dependencies

**Governance**
• Unified security policies
• Shared incident response
• Integrated risk management
• Joint security operations

**Best Practices:**
• Network segmentation
• Zero trust architecture
• OT-specific monitoring
• Joint training programs

sections.title: IoT/OT Audit Considerations
sections.content:
**Audit Focus Areas:**

**Asset Management**
• Complete device inventory
• Firmware version tracking
• End-of-life identification
• Vendor relationship

**Network Security**
• Segmentation verification
• Traffic monitoring
• Protocol analysis
• Remote access controls

**Incident Response**
• OT-specific playbooks
• Safety procedures
• Recovery priorities
• Vendor escalation

**Compliance**
• NERC CIP (energy)
• IEC 62443 (industrial)
• NIST CSF mapping
• Sector-specific requirements

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: OT prioritizes availability and safety over confidentiality
IoT devices have constraints limiting traditional security controls
Purdue Model provides framework for ICS network segmentation
IT/OT convergence requires unified governance and specialized skills
OT audits must consider safety, availability, and long device lifecycles

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is the MOST significant security concern arising from the use of default credentials on IoT devices?
options: Increased battery consumption due to encryption overhead.
Unauthorized access and control of the device and potentially the network.
Difficulty in applying security updates due to vendor restrictions.
Limited processing power affecting the device's operational performance.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Default credentials are a well-known vulnerability that allows attackers to easily gain unauthorized access to the device. This can lead to compromise of the device, data theft, and further exploitation of the network it is connected to.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the immediate consequences of using a common, easily guessable password.

reveal.question: What security measures are MOST critical at Level 0-1 of the Purdue Model?
reveal.answer: Physical security is paramount at Levels 0-1 (Field Devices). While cyber controls are limited, protecting sensors, actuators, and PLCs from physical tampering is crucial. Cyber controls are limited due to device constraints.

rows.label: Impact of Security Breach
values: Data breach, financial loss, reputational damage
Physical damage, safety risks, environmental impact

rows.label: Update Frequency
values: Less frequent updates due to operational constraints

title: API Security
description: Security for Application Programming Interfaces and web services

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: APIs are the backbone of modern applications and integrations. API vulnerabilities have led to major data breaches. Auditors must understand API-specific risks and controls as organizations expose more functionality through APIs.

sections.title: OWASP API Security Top 10 (2023)
rows: Accessing other users resources by manipulating IDs
Exposing or modifying sensitive object properties
No rate limiting or resource controls
Accessing admin functions without proper checks

sections.title: API Authentication Methods
sections.content:
**Authentication Options:**

**API Keys**
• Simple implementation
• Limited security
• No user context
• Best for internal/low-risk

**OAuth 2.0**
• Industry standard
• Delegated authorization
• Scoped access
• Token-based

**JWT (JSON Web Tokens)**
• Self-contained claims
• Stateless validation
• Expiration built-in
• Signature verification

**mTLS (Mutual TLS)**
• Certificate-based
• Strong authentication
• Service-to-service
• Complex management

**Best Practice:**
• Use OAuth 2.0 + JWT for user access
• Use mTLS for service-to-service
• Never expose API keys client-side

sections.title: API Gateway Security
sections.content:
**Gateway Security Functions:**

**Traffic Management**
• Rate limiting
• Throttling
• Request/response transformation
• Load balancing

**Security**
• Authentication/authorization
• Input validation
• TLS termination
• WAF integration

**Monitoring**
• Logging all requests
• Anomaly detection
• Usage analytics
• SLA monitoring

**Policy Enforcement**
• API versioning
• Deprecation management
• Consumer quotas
• IP whitelisting

sections.title: API Security Controls
sections.content:
**Essential Controls:**

**Input Validation**
• Schema validation
• Size limits
• Type checking
• Content-type enforcement

**Output Security**
• Data filtering
• Pagination
• Error message sanitization
• Response headers

**Rate Limiting**
• Per-user limits
• Per-endpoint limits
• Burst handling
• Retry-after headers

**Logging & Monitoring**
• Full request logging
• Sensitive data masking
• Anomaly detection
• Alerting thresholds

sections.title: API Audit Checklist
sections.content:
**Audit Focus Areas:**

**Authentication & Authorization**
• Token validation implementation
• Scope enforcement
• BOLA/BFLA testing
• Session management

**Data Protection**
• Encryption in transit (TLS 1.2+)
• Sensitive data exposure
• Response filtering
• Field-level authorization

**Availability**
• Rate limiting configuration
• DDoS protection
• Resource quotas
• Graceful degradation

**Documentation & Governance**
• OpenAPI specifications
• API inventory
• Deprecation process
• Change management

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: API Security TRAP
sections.content:
**T-R-A-P:**
• **T**okens - Secure authentication/authorization
• **R**ate limiting - Prevent abuse
• **A**uthorization - Object/function level checks
• **P**rotection - Input validation, output filtering

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: OWASP API Top 10 highlights authorization and authentication as top risks
OAuth 2.0 + JWT is recommended for user API access; mTLS for services
API gateways provide centralized security, monitoring, and policy enforcement
Rate limiting and input validation are essential abuse prevention controls
API audits should verify authorization at object, property, and function levels

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following authentication methods provides delegated authorization, scoped access, and is considered an industry standard for API security?
options: API Keys
JWT (JSON Web Tokens)
OAuth 2.0
Basic Authentication
knowledgeCheck.explanation: OAuth 2.0 is the industry standard for delegated authorization, allowing users to grant limited access to their resources without sharing their credentials. API keys are simpler but lack user context and scoped access. JWTs are for stateless validation of claims, not delegated authorization. Basic Authentication is generally discouraged due to its lack of security features.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about which method is designed for allowing one application to access resources on behalf of a user, without sharing credentials.

sections.title: API Gateway Security Functions - Test Yourself

reveal.question: Name three essential security functions performed by an API Gateway.
reveal.answer:
1. Authentication/Authorization: Verifying the identity and permissions of API requesters.
2. Input Validation: Ensuring that API requests conform to expected formats and constraints, preventing injection attacks.
3. TLS Termination: Decrypting HTTPS traffic, improving performance and security by centralizing certificate management.
4. WAF Integration: Filtering malicious traffic through integration with a Web Application Firewall.

rows.label: Use Case
values: External APIs, user data access, high-risk scenarios

knowledgeCheck.question: An auditor is reviewing the API security controls for a financial institution. Which of the following controls would be MOST effective in preventing sensitive data leakage in API responses?
options: Schema validation
Content-type enforcement
Data filtering
Rate limiting
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Data filtering is the most effective control for preventing sensitive data leakage in API responses. It ensures that only necessary and authorized data is included in the response, preventing unintended exposure of sensitive information. Schema validation focuses on input validation. Content-type enforcement helps prevent content-based attacks. Rate limiting prevents abuse but does not directly address data leakage.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider which control directly manipulates the data being sent in the API response.
